# Exploring Neural Machine Translation (NMT)

Explore the revolutionary world of Neural Machine Translation (NMT) and its impact on the localization industry. Learn how NMT works, its pros and cons, and its future potential in language translation.

Table of Contents

[What is Neural Machine Translation (NMT)?](#what-is-neural-machine-translation-nmt)

[How Neural Machine Translation (NMT) Works](#how-neural-machine-translation-nmt-works)

[The Architecture of Neural Machine Translation (NMT)](#the-architecture-of-neural-machine-translation-nmt)

[The Pros and Cons of Neural Machine Translation (NMT)](#the-pros-and-cons-of-neural-machine-translation-nmt)

[Is NMT the Future of Language Translation?](#is-nmt-the-future-of-language-translation)

[Neural Machine Translation and Localization](#neural-machine-translation-and-localization)

[Why Use Localize?](#why-use-localize)

[Talk to an Expert](/request-demo/book-a-30-minute-product-demo)

Share

Did you know that computers can be trained to understand and predict patterns? This is especially true for patterns in words and language.

Computers are now smart enough to translate an entire novel in less than two minutes. With machine learning (a form of artificial intelligence), programmers use statistical techniques to give computers the ability to learn different languages. The biggest trend in this linguistic tech is **neural machine translation (NMT)**. NMT has the potential to revolutionize the localization industry.

## **What is Neural Machine Translation (NMT)?**

NMT is a developing AI technology that uses deep learning to translate sentences from one language to another. Like the human brain, NMT gets smarter over time by building connections. For example, NMT can be trained to understand that the word “car” is related to “auto,” “automobile,” “vehicle,” and “cars.” Older methods of machine translation can’t build connections like this.

Before NMT came along, machine translation engines used a word-for-word approach to translation. These translations often felt stilted and robotic. NMT is smart and accurate enough to generate high-quality translations that feel more human and realistic.

This translation technology is constantly evolving, so you might be wondering if NMT will replace human translators anytime soon. Let’s explore NMT and find out.

## **How Neural Machine Translation (NMT) Works**

Think about how humans comprehend language. When we want to translate ‘The girl rides the bike,’ our brain first makes a mental representation of what that looks like as a whole. Once we have a full mental picture, we can proceed to translate. Neural MT works similarly.

Neural machine translation translates entire sentences at a time, rather than word-by-word or phrase-by-phrase like older [machine translation](/articles/types-of-machine-translation) models. NMT uses the context of a sentence to come up with the most relevant translation. It then makes adjustments and rearrangements until the translation reads more like human speech with correct grammar.

Classic examples of publicly accessible NMT software are Google Translate and Baidu Translate. Many multinational corporations are utilizing NMT to help them overcome translation challenges related to their global expansion efforts.

Google Translate is the most widely used machine translation tool around the globe—translating over 140 billion words per day. The [Google Neural Machine Translation System (GNMTS)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1609.08144) significantly increases translation accuracy and fluency. The tech giant uses enormous data sets to train its translation algorithms and has an end-to-end design to boost learning over time.

## **The Architecture of Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**

An NMT computer uses [deep learning](https://opensource.com/article/17/9/representation-learning) to build an artificial neural network to teach it how to translate between languages. This computational system strives to mimic the billions of neuron cells in the human brain that use observable data to learn and make decisions. These neural networks translate entire sentences without breaking them down into smaller parts. Neural machine translation can also predict the likelihood of a sequence of words.

To do this, NMT uses an [encoder-attention-decoder](https://towardsdatascience.com/understanding-neural-machine-translation-encoder-decoder-architecture-80f205643ba4) model. An encoding neural network is capable of reading and encoding a source sentence into a ‘meaning vector’ or ‘thought vector.’ This vector is a number sequence that represents the meaning of the sentence. A decoder then produces a translation from the encoded vector.

The encoder-decoder system is trained with large amounts of data to maximize the probability of producing a correct translation from a source sentence. This enables it to capture dependencies in languages, such as syntax structures and gender agreement, resulting in much more fluent translations.

Neural machine translation is a single, well-trained system that can accurately translate source text to a target language. It does away with the need to use specialized systems that are typical of statistical machine learning. Its biggest strength is its ability to perform end-to-end direct learning.

NMT applies to pretty much any language, including ones that are comprehended by relatively few people. Users can fine-tune translations to suit specific types and styles of languages, e.g., UK-English, US-English, and medical, financial, and scientific languages. Several industries requiring custom-developed and high-accuracy machine translation use a blend of statistical and neural translation methods.

## **The Pros and Cons of Neural Machine Translation (NMT)**

NMT is a promising technology, but it’s far from perfect. Here are some big advantages and disadvantages of neural machine translation.

#### Advantages of NMT






**Natural-sounding language**

* **‍**NMT can make AI-translated language sound more human and less robotic. Here’s a great example from Google:



**Context**

* **‍**NMT isn’t perfect, but it does a good job of interpreting [translation context](/articles/translation-context-why-it-matters). Google made recent strides in training its NMT to pay greater attention to the [context of a sentence](https://wordbee.com/blog/localization-industry/trends-in-neural-machine-translation/) within a body of work.

**Generalization**

* **‍**NMT gets smarter over time. Like the human brain, it can generalize to make new [conclusions and connections](https://omniscien.com/blog/state-neural-machine-translation-nmt/). It uses these connections to quickly learn different language pairs.

**Good accuracy in some contexts**

* **‍**NMT is especially good at translating repetitive content that requires high accuracy like manuals, guides, or reference materials.

**Speed**

* **‍**Once an NMT system is trained, the [translation speed](https://www.systransoft.com/blog/nmt-scaling-four-ways-create-translation-powerhouse/) is impressive. NMT can translate Charles Dickens’ *A Tale of Two Cities* in a little over a minute.

**Flexibility and integrations**

* **‍**Another [big advantage](https://www.memsource.com/blog/neural-machine-translation/): NMT can be easily integrated into software with APIs and SDKs.

#### Disadvantages of NMT






**Need for clarity in the source text**

* **‍**Source text needs to be very clear for NMT to generate a quality translation. Neural machine translation has [difficulties](https://www.textmaster.com/blog/neural-machine-translation-guide/) with ambiguities, highly technical language, proper nouns, and rare words.

**Poor translation of long sentences**

* **‍**NMT generally outperforms other machine translation methods, but there’s an interesting exception. NMT can't translate [long sentences](https://aclanthology.org/K19-1031/) very well.

**Large data sets needed**

* **‍**For smaller projects, NMT isn’t always a good fit. NMT systems produce comparably poor results with small data sets. Without a large amount of training data, NMT isn’t going to give you accurate translations.

**Expertise limitations**

* **‍**NMT can’t perform well without the expertise and cognitive power of human architects and engineers. Any NMT model must be trained with a large quantity of linguistic data.

**Human post-editing still needed**

* **‍**NMT text should always be checked by a human editor. Language interpretation requires a level of critical thinking and nuance that computers haven’t achieved yet.

## **Is NMT the Future of Language Translation?**

As the field of [computational linguistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computational_linguistics) evolves, we have a lot to look forward to. NMT technology continues to get better and better. NMT’s greatest potential is being a timesaver for translation and localization teams, with the potential to reduce the time needed for post-editing by up to [25%](https://www.adverbum.com/en/blog/nmt).

AI industry pioneer [Philipp Koehn](https://omniscien.com/blog/state-neural-machine-translation-nmt/) agrees that NMT is incredibly exciting, but he also encourages realistic expectations:

> "It is clear that Neural Machine Translation (NMT) is yet another step up and a very promising technology. At the same time, as often with new technologies, we are currently in the 'hype' phase and while NMT might be right for some applications today, it is important to understand the different technologies and their respective advantages and disadvantages..."

It seems that NMT still has a long way to go before it surpasses the translation performance of people. At least for now, the human brain can’t be beaten!

## **Neural Machine Translation and Localization**

As more companies go global, [localization](/articles/what-is-localization) teams work to meet the demand for localized content in international markets. NMT reduces translation costs and helps translators produce content more quickly. However, until AI translations can 100% imitate the human brain, [post-editing from human translators](/articles/importance-post-editing-machine-translation) will always be needed.

## Why Use *Localize*?

*Localize* is a [translation management system](/articles/what-is-a-translation-management-system) that is always keeping an eye on future technical developments that can improve the quality of translations. *Localize* provides several cutting-edge machine translation options including Google Translate, Amazon Translate, and Microsoft Translate. --- SOURCE 2: What is Neural Machine Translation & How does it work? ---

SUMMARY:
Neural machine translation (NMT) is typically software used to translate words from one language to another.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/packs/media/src/images/company/med-logo-b114117101cc596445d48ae05c10c896.png)
![](/packs/media/src/images/company/med-logo-b114117101cc596445d48ae05c10c896.png)

# What is Neural Machine Translation & How does it work?

*Published on 18 Apr 11:10 by **Sam Yip**
  
Tags:
[translation](/blog?category=translation)  
[AI](/blog?category=AI)  
[machine translation](/blog?category=machine translation)  
[tools](/blog?category=tools)*

![TranslateFX What is Neural Machine Translation & How does it work?](/rails/active_storage/representations/eyJfcmFpbHMiOnsibWVzc2FnZSI6IkJBaHBFZz09IiwiZXhwIjpudWxsLCJwdXIiOiJibG9iX2lkIn19--1f301155a359c76ecaf7a0150e21487383439a1b/eyJfcmFpbHMiOnsibWVzc2FnZSI6IkJBaDdCam9VWTI5dFltbHVaVjl2Y0hScGIyNXpld2M2QzNKbGMybDZaVWtpRGpFeU1EQjRNVEl3TUFZNkJrVlVPZ3huY21GMmFYUjVTU0lMWTJWdWRHVnlCanNIVkE9PSIsImV4cCI6bnVsbCwicHVyIjoidmFyaWF0aW9uIn19--b79590883e9424ae568730e38cda3731410b9bc0/NMT.png "TranslateFX What is Neural Machine Translation & How does it work?")

Neural machine translation (NMT) is typically software used to translate words from one language to another. [Google Translate](https://translate.google.com/), [Baidu Translate](https://fanyi.baidu.com/) are well-known examples of NMT offered to the public via the Internet.

The reason this NMT is important is because recent advancements in the technology have allowed an increasing number of multinational institutions to adopt NMT engines to aid in internal and external communications.

### 

![](https://www.translatefx.com/rails/active_storage/blobs/eyJfcmFpbHMiOnsibWVzc2FnZSI6IkJBaHBGUT09IiwiZXhwIjpudWxsLCJwdXIiOiJibG9iX2lkIn19--f9541ad3c604b0c5fc678191dcdd3ad54d84d5a6/A-brief-history-of-MT.jpg?lang=en)

As you can see in the chart above, neural machine translation technology is currently *state-of-the-art* technology in machine translation and offers the highest quality translation. [Google Translate started using the technology to power its service in 2016](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Neural_Machine_Translation) after having used statistical methods for many years.

### How Does It Work?

Technically, NMTs encompass all types of machine translation where an artificial [neural network](https://pathmind.com/wiki/neural-network) is used to predict a sequence of numbers when provided with a sequence of numbers. In the case of translation, each word in the input sentence (e.g English) is encoded as a number to be translated by the neural network into a resulting sequence of numbers representing the translated target sentence (e.g Chinese).

To give you a simplified example of an English to Chinese machine translation:

*"I am a dog" is encoded into numbers 251, 3245, 953, 2*

*The numbers 251, 3245, 953, 2 are input into a neural translation model and results in output 2241, 9242, 98, 6342*

*2241, 9242, 98, 6342 is then decoded into the Chinese translation “我是只狗"*

*(each number in the input and output represents a word in the English and Chinese dictionary and are always encoded and decoded accordingly)*

The above example then begs a further question 'How does the translation model work?' The simple answer is it is via a complex mathematical formula (represented as a neural network). As described earlier, this formula takes in a string of numbers as inputs and outputs a resulting string of numbers. The parameters of this neural network are created and refined via training the network with millions of sentence pairs (e.g English and Chinese sentence pair translations). Each sentence pair modifies the neural network slightly as it runs through each sentence pair using an algorithm called [back-propagation](https://towardsdatascience.com/how-does-back-propagation-in-artificial-neural-networks-work-c7cad873ea7?gi=99d63e50de6). This results in a best-fit model most accurately translating any of the input numbers into outputs numbers from the millions of sentence pairs it was provided.

### Why Neural Networks?

![](https://www.translatefx.com/rails/active_storage/blobs/eyJfcmFpbHMiOnsibWVzc2FnZSI6IkJBaHBHQT09IiwiZXhwIjpudWxsLCJwdXIiOiJibG9iX2lkIn19--da1dd3adfa32d74f53915030224e57eebff7d9f3/deep-neural-networks.png?lang=en)

![](https://www.translatefx.com/rails/active_storage/blobs/eyJfcmFpbHMiOnsibWVzc2FnZSI6IkJBaHBHQT09IiwiZXhwIjpudWxsLCJwdXIiOiJibG9iX2lkIn19--da1dd3adfa32d74f53915030224e57eebff7d9f3/deep-neural-networks.png?lang=en)

Simply put, neural networks permit complexity. Neural networks can have a large number of parameters with weights and biases between nodes to give them the flexibility to fit highly complex data and train complex models. Model complexity allows the model to generalize to the large volumes of examples it’s trained on, such as digesting millions of language pairs.

One way to understand neural networks is to think of the input as a signal with "information" in it. Some of this information is relevant for the task at hand (e.g predicting the output). Think of the output as also a signal with a certain amount of "information". The neural network attempts to "repeatedly refine" and compress the input signal's information to match the desired output signal. Think of each hidden layer of the network as stripping out unimportant parts of the input information, and keeping and/or transforming the output information along the way through the network. The fully-connected neural network will transform the input information into a form in the final hidden layer, such that it is separable by the output layer.

### 

### Use Cases of NMT

NMT technology can be applied to any language pair including languages that are brand new or understood by few. They can also be fine-tuned accordingly to particular styles and types of a languages (e.g casual, formal, US-English, UK-English, scientific, medical, financial, etc..). NMTs are primarily dependent on the training data used to train the neural network as it learns to mimic the data it has been trained with. Many high-accuracy industry specific and custom developed machine translation (MT) models still incorporate both neural and statistical methods today to squeeze the best performance for our clients (including the ones we develop at [TranslateFX](https://www.translatefx.com/enterprise)).

### Lasting Remarks

One important disadvantage worth mentioning regarding NMT is consistency. Since neural networks are trained to learn upon volumes of data, they are not easy to tame and control. It may translate a particular team 'restaurant' as ‘菜馆‘ in one scenario and as ’餐馆‘ in another scenario (both correct Chinese translations). This becomes particularly troublesome when it comes to translating names (people or companies). At [TranslateFX](https://www.translatefx.com/enterprise), we had to develop additional neural models to overcome such faults of NMT.

It is also important to mention that while the benefits of NMT have made it an indispensable part of translation management and workflow, NMTs still require human translators to review and post-edit of machine translation between languages. In fact, we believe humans will always play a critical step in machine translation to review the accuracy and context of machine. I'll further explore this topic next time as it worth a post of its own.

# Why use neural machine translation and where can you start?

Neural machine translation can deliver high-quality translations in less time and for a fraction of the cost of human translation. Learn more about it here.

For decades, scientists and engineers have been working hard to develop the full potential of neural machine translation (NMT). While NMT models like those used by Microsoft Translator have transformed how we communicate globally, the translation industry is now gravitating towards new advancements, like Small Language Models (SMLs) or Large Language Models (LLMs) fine-tuned specifically for translation tasks. For example, Google now positions its new [Gemini-based Translation LLM](https://console.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/publishers/google/model-garden/translate-llm?pli=1&inv=1&invt=Abm7vw) as a solution for a wide range of translation use cases over its commonly-used NMT model, Google Translate.

Despite these advancements, NMT still plays a role in translation today. Whether instantly translating road signs from a picture or interpreting business and legal documents, NMT has proven indispensable across numerous industries. Companies that leverage the power of machine translation and artificial intelligence (AI) save time, break language barriers, and reach international markets faster than their competitors that use traditional translation methods.

Here, we’ll define neural machine translation, how it works, and when to use it for your language translation needs to improve global reach.

## What is neural machine translation?

Neural machine translation uses neural networks to provide accurate translations in various languages. Computer programs that employ NMT automatically translate text from one language to another, leveraging patterns learned from large datasets.

Not long ago, NMT was viewed by many as an inconsistent [translation tool](https://www.smartling.com/resources/101/ai-translation-tools/) for translating important or high-visibility content, especially at scale. However, its ability to capture subtle linguistic nuances has drastically improved over the past decade. Now, it’s a go-to method for translating text, revolutionizing our approach to language translation and localization.

For example, [AI translation software](https://www.smartling.com/resources/101/ai-translation-software/), such as Smartling’s neural machine translation hub, uses machine learning to select the best MT engine, ensuring accurate translation results in milliseconds. Smartling provides a central workspace to process multilingual content across various translation methods, workflows, and file formats. It’s an invaluable translation tool for international businesses that must communicate in different languages, producing high-quality translations you can trust.

### How does neural machine translation work?

Neural machine translation uses artificial neural networks to mimic the human translation process at a much faster pace. These networks comprise layers of interconnected nodes that encode the source text, decode it into the target language, and employ an attention mechanism to ensure contextually accurate translations. NMT models learn from vast training datasets in each language.

### What is a neural network?

A neural network is a computer system designed to mimic how the human brain processes and learns information. It’s made up of interconnected nodes, or “neurons,” that learn by passing information between nodes and adjusting connections based on training data to minimize prediction errors. This process teaches the network how to interpret and react to data, optimizing its performance for an assigned task, such as translating text.

### What is deep learning translation?

Deep learning translation  is an advanced branch of AI that specifically utilizes deep neural networks. Unlike traditional or “shallow” NMT models that use just one or a few layers, deep learning uses many layers to analyze and learn from language data. This depth allows for a richer understanding of nuances, significantly [improving translation accuracy](https://www.smartling.com/resources/101/5-strategies-for-achieving-accurate-technical-translations/) and naturalness.

## Different types of NMT models

There are two primary types of neural machine translation models, each with a unique approach to translating languages:

### Transformer models

Transformer models process all words in a sentence at the same time. They use a system called self-attention, which weighs each word in reference to every other word in the sentence. This makes it easy for these models to understand relationships between words, even if they are far apart. After processing the words, they pass the information through feedforward layers to refine their understanding. The ability to process words in parallel makes transformers much faster and more powerful than older models.

Transformers can even translate in “zero-shot” scenarios, meaning they can translate between language pairs they haven’t been trained on by drawing from training data on each individual language. This makes them flexible and good at predicting translations across numerous language combinations.

### RNN models

Recurrent neural networks (RNN) process words sequentially, meaning they look at one word at a time and pass information from previous words forward. This helps them understand patterns over time, but it also means they struggle with long sentences because they can “forget” earlier words. Since RNNs process information step by step, they are also much slower than transformers and can’t handle large datasets. RNN models are rarely used anymore as they have been replaced by transformers, which are far more accurate and efficient. 

## When to use neural machine translation

Neural machine translation has evolved over the years to suit countless use cases. Here are some of its most common applications in business environments:

### Streamlined workplace communication

NMT simplifies communication between employees and departments in global companies by translating emails, messages, and internal documents. Everyone stays on the same page, no matter where they’re from or what language they speak. It’s a great way to build a stronger, more connected team.

### Real-time customer support

Global companies must handle customer inquiries from every market in which they operate. NMT enables real-time translation of customer inquiries and support responses, allowing companies to instantly assist customers in their preferred languages. International operations no longer require a multilingual support team.

### Major translation projects

NMT can translate entire websites, user manuals, and other documents cheaper and faster than human translators. AI translation tools like Smartling’s [Neural Machine Translation Hub](https://www.smartling.com/software/neural-machine-translation-hub/) streamline the process, providing high-quality translations in no time. Smartling’s NMT hub is ideal for website localization or updates to multilingual content repositories.

### Translations of specialized content

Translations must uphold strict regulations in sensitive industries like healthcare or finance and preserve technical jargon. NMT systems can be trained on domain-specific data, easily translating complex or sensitive content while keeping the original meaning.

## Advantages of neural machine translation

There are numerous benefits to integrating neural machine translation into your translation strategy.

## One centralized hub for smarter machine translation

You can’t rely on a one-size-fits-all solution to [scale your localization efforts](https://www.smartling.com/resources/101/scaling-localization-through-constraint/). Machine translation engines excel in specialized areas. Some are ideal for niche industries or domains, whereas others perform better with specific language pairs or content types. Limiting yourself to a single engine means missing out on the full potential of AI-powered translation.

With Smartling’s [MT Auto Select](https://www.smartling.com/resources/product/choosing-the-best-mt-engines-with-mt-auto-select-webinar/), you don’t have to settle for one solution. Integrated within our Neural Machine Translation Hub, Auto Select gives you access to top engines like DeepL, Microsoft Translator, and Google Translate—all working together to deliver fast, accurate, and scalable results. By automatically selecting the best machine translation engine for your content and locale pair, Auto Select yields 350% higher-quality translations to improve your [multilingual SEO](https://www.smartling.com/resources/101/is-ai-good-for-seo/) and branded communication.

Today’s translation tech spans far beyond just NMT. The next generation of translation solutions, like [Smartling’s AI Translation](https://www.smartling.com/software/ai-translation/), leverage both NMT and LLMs to achieve even more efficient, fluent results.

enriched_points.expanded_content: NMT employs deep learning and neural networks to translate entire sentences by encoding input text into vectors, then decoding them to generate fluent output, mimicking human understanding.
NMT surpasses Statistical Machine Translation (SMT) by providing more natural, context-aware translations, reducing errors associated with word-for-word translation and capturing nuanced meanings.
NMT utilizes contextual information from entire sentences, enhancing translation accuracy by considering relationships between words, resulting in more coherent and meaningful translations.
NMT struggles with ambiguous texts, long sentences, and requires extensive training data, making it less effective for niche topics or languages with limited resources.
NMT demands significant computational resources, including powerful GPUs and large datasets, which can be cost-prohibitive for smaller projects or organizations.
